content.value: A stack only allows access at one end (the top): push adds an element, pop removes the most recently added one. Both are O(1).

items: Infix: operator between operands, e.g. A + B (needs precedence/parentheses rules)
Prefix (Polish): operator before operands, e.g. + A B
Postfix (Reverse Polish): operator after operands, e.g. A B +

content.value: Computers evaluate postfix and prefix expressions trivially with a stack, without needing precedence rules or parentheses - this is why compilers convert infix expressions internally.
Scan left to right: push operands onto the stack; when an operator is found, pop the top two operands, apply the operator, and push the result back.

Use an operator stack and an output queue/list. Operands go straight to output. Operators are pushed onto the stack after popping (to output) any operators on the stack with greater or equal precedence. Parentheses control grouping: '(' is pushed, and ')' pops operators until the matching '(' is found.
When converting infix to postfix by hand for an exam, build a small table tracking the stack contents and output after each token - graders give partial credit for showing this trace.

quiz.question: A stack follows which access order?
Why do compilers prefer postfix/prefix over infix internally?
options: They can be evaluated with a simple stack scan, without precedence rules

quiz.question: In postfix evaluation, what do you do when you encounter an operator?
options: Pop the top two operands, apply the operator, push the result
